title: Psychometric properties of the Dysexecutive Questionnaire (DEX) in individuals with a previous cardiac event
abstract: This psychometric study aimed to evaluate the psychometric properties of the Dysexecutive Questionnaire (DEX) following a cardiac event, and to examine the relationship between self- and informant-ratings. Approximately 7 months post-cardiac event, 196 participants and their informants (N = 179) completed the DEX. Reliability was assessed by confirmatory factor analysis and internal consistency estimates. Construct validity was evaluated using two performance-based neuropsychological tests, the Colour-Word Interference Test and the Trail Making Test (TMT), along with the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS). A one-factor model showed acceptable fit for the self-assessed DEX (RMSEA = 0.062) with excellent internal consistency (ω > 0.90). Slight positive associations were found between the DEX and the performance-based executive function measures, while strong positive associations emerged between the self-assessed DEX and the HADS. Overall, self-assessed and informant-reported scores did not differ significantly (self-assessed DEX median = 11, Q1–Q3 = 5.75–20; informant-reported DEX median = 11, Q1–Q3 = 4–19, p = 0.924). However, participants with severe executive dysfunction (TMT-B z-scores ≤−2) rated their own symptoms as less severe than informants (p =.001), indicating impaired self-awareness in a subgroup of participants. In conclusion, the DEX provides a useful perspective into general executive dysfunction in daily life but should be complemented with performance-based measures. Trial registration:ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03543371.

Introduction
Individuals with previous cardiac events, such as myocardial infarction (MI) and 
out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA), show variability in cognitive functioning, 
particularly in executive function (EF; Blennow Nordström et al., 2023; Byron- 
Alhassan et al., 2021; Mion et al., 2023; Zook et al., 2022). Executive functions 
(EFs) refer to top-down mental processes – including inhibitory control, 
working memory and cognitive flexibility – that are critical for complex, goal- 
directed behaviours such as problem solving, reasoning, and planning 
(Diamond, 2013). EF closely relates to processing speed, which may facilitate 
or hinder the demonstration of EF abilities, especially in older adults 
(Diamond, 2013; Salthouse, 1992).
Cardiovascular burden is generally associated with an increased risk of cog­
nitive impairment as a consequence of atherosclerosis, arteriosclerosis, and 
other vascular risk factors (Qiu & Fratiglioni, 2015). Long-term cardiovascular 
burden often precedes MI and has been linked to poorer performance on 
tasks measuring attention, processing speed, EF, learning and memory (Stefani­
dis et al., 2018). OHCA may result in acquired brain injury due to hypoxic- 
ischemic injury, leading to further cognitive decline (Blennow Nordström et 
al., 2023; Howell et al., 2013). Cognitive impairments following OHCA include 
memory, attention, processing speed, EF and language (Byron-Alhassan et al., 
2021; Zook et al., 2022). Lower EF abilities in particular are associated with 
poorer treatment adherence (Gaalema et al., 2019; Insel et al., 2006), highlight­
ing the need for reliable and convenient assessment tools to capture executive 
difficulties in affected clinical populations.
One example of a commonly used rating scale for executive problems is the 
Dysexecutive Questionnaire (DEX; Burgess et al., 1996), originally developed as a 
tool to sample common complaints among patients with acquired brain injury. 
The DEX is used quantitatively in both clinical practice and research in a variety 
of patient groups due to its brevity, ease of administration, and earlier research 
showing promising ecological validity (Burgess et al., 1998). The 20-item rating 
scale is aimed to assess symptoms of executive dysfunction related to emotions, 
behaviours, motivation, and cognition (Burgess et al., 1996), and is available 
2 
A. VIG ET AL.
both as a self-assessment (DEX-Self) and in an informant-rating format (DEX- 
Informant).
Research on the psychometric properties of DEX is limited. Although existing 
studies have focused on different populations (e.g., acquired brain injury, psy­
chiatric, non-clinical), none have examined its reliability and validity in individ­
uals with cardiovascular risk. Analyzes regarding the latent structure of the DEX 
have yielded inconsistent results, with different methodologies and sample 
types producing factor solutions ranging from one to five (Burgess et al., 
1998; Hellebrekers et al., 2017; McGuire et al., 2014; Pedrero-Pérez et al., 2015; 
Simblett & Bateman, 2011). Even studies employing methods appropriate for 
ordinal categorical measures have resulted in different factor structures: Sim­
blett and Bateman (2011) concluded that a three-factor model best fits the 
DEX when looking at a sample of individuals with acquired brain injury, 
whereas Pedrero-Pérez et al. (2015) supported a unidimensional solution in 
both a non-clinical sample and an outpatient sample receiving treatment for 
substance use-related problems.
With regards to construct validity, findings on associations between DEX 
scores and performance-based test results have been mixed, varying between 
no associations to moderate associations (Bennett et al., 2005; Boelen et al., 
2009; Chaytor et al., 2006). Population effects may have influenced these vari­
able findings according to Evans et al. (1997), who found significant associations 
between performance-based assessments and the DEX in a brain-injured 
sample, but not in patients with schizophrenia. Furthermore, anxiety and 
depression have both been associated with greater impairment of EF as 
measured by the DEX, though these relationships have only been scarcely 
explored (Azouvi et al., 2015; Gerstorf et al., 2008; Shaw et al., 2015). Depression 
and anxiety are common among MI and OHCA survivors and are thought to 
result both from physiological changes and of psychosocial and functional chal­
lenges that follow the cardiac event (Chong et al., 2025; Yaow et al., 2022). 
Symptoms of anxiety, depression and executive dysfunction may overlap, e.g., 
problems with decision making could be due to excessive and persistent 
anxiety, or anhedonia and indecisiveness as in major depressive disorder, or 
impaired initiation and higher-order EFs. Moreover, EF problems might not 
only result from neurological damage; some studies report that anxiety and 
depressive disorders could predispose people to future decreased EF 
(Hammar et al., 2022; Zainal & Newman, 2022). Hence, accurate characterization 
is essential for delivering individualized rehabilitation and may enhance under­
standing of the latent structure of the DEX.
A handful of studies have examined the discrepancy between self- and infor­
mant-rated DEX scores as a means of assessing self-awareness. Some research 
suggests that individuals with acquired brain injury tend to underreport the 
severity of their EF impairment as measured by the DEX (Barker et al., 2011; 
Burgess et al., 1998; McGuire et al., 2014), while other studies have not found 
NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL REHABILITATION 
3
any discrepancies in self- and informant-assessed total scores (Boelen et al., 
2009; Chan & Bode, 2008; Lesimple et al., 2020). Prior research shows that 
OHCA survivors may underreport symptom severity as measured by other 
assessments (van Gils et al., 2022), while not much is known about potential 
issues with self-awareness of cognitive changes in survivors of acute MI. Captur­
ing impaired self-awareness of EF is however of value as greater self-awareness 
is related to better rehabilitation outcomes and increased functional indepen­
dence (Ownsworth & Clare, 2006; Robertson & Schmitter-Edgecombe, 2015; Vil­
lalobos et al., 2020).
Because the ecological validity of EF measurements may vary depending on type 
and severity of impairment, it is essential to conduct research across populations 
(Chaytor & Schmitter-Edgecombe, 2003). In the current study, cohorts of individuals 
with a history of MI or OHCA were merged to form a sample with cardiovascular 
burden that is sufficiently sized for validation purposes (Clark & Watson, 1995).
In our combined sample of individuals with a previous cardiac event (i.e., MI 
or OHCA), this study aims to: 
(1) Evaluate the hypothesized one-factor structure (structural validity) and the 
internal consistency reliability of the DEX.
(2) Assess construct validity by investigating the associations between the DEX 
and neuropsychological EF test results, as well as between the DEX and 
symptoms of anxiety and depression.
(3) Examine the relationship between self- and informant-ratings on the DEX.
Materials and methods
Participants
Participants with a previous OHCA of cardiac or unknown cause were recruited 
within the international multicenter trial Targeted Hypothermia versus Targeted 
Normothermia after Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (TTM2; Dankiewicz et al., 
2021; Lilja et al., 2023). OHCA and acute MI participants in Sweden, Denmark, 
and the United Kingdom were included within the scope of the sub-study Neu­
ropsychological Outcome After Cardiac Arrest (Blennow Nordström et al., 2020, 
2023). Sub-study exclusion criteria were need of interpreter, age 80 years or 
older, clinical diagnosis with a major neurocognitive disorder (dementia) prior 
to the cardiac event, ongoing substance abuse, severe frailty, or an inability 
to meet for a face-to-face examination. An additional inclusion criterion for 
the present analysis was a completed DEX. Initial results from the sub-study 
showed that 29% of OHCA and 16% of MI participants exhibited at least border­
line to mild impairment defined as z-score performance of ≤−1 in two or more 
cognitive domains, but the cohorts only exhibited a slight difference in EF abil­
ities based on performance-based tests (Blennow Nordström et al., 2023).
4

Procedure
Ethical approval was obtained from the corresponding ethics commissions in all 
participating countries. Participants received written information about the 
study as well as of their rights prior to signing a consent form. Approximately 
7 months after the cardiac event, participants underwent a full neuropsycholo­
gical assessment, which included completing the DEX-Self as part of the neu­
ropsychological sub-study. The neuropsychological examination took place in 
a hospital setting or in the participants’ home and was administered by neurop­
sychologists, psychologists, assistant clinical psychologists, or psychology stu­
dents under supervision. A battery of neuropsychological tests was 
administered of which a selection relevant to the current study will be reported. 
Rating scales were completed briefly before or after the neuropsychological 
examination. Participants were instructed to allot the DEX-Informant to some­
body who knew them well (e.g., a relative, a significant other or a close 
friend) and to complete the questionnaires privately and confidentially.
Measures
Dysexecutive Questionnaire (DEX). The DEX (Burgess et al., 1996) consists of 
20 items that assess the frequency of executive difficulties in daily life. It can be 
completed by participants rating their own experiences, or by informants pro­
viding their perceptions of the participant’s difficulties. Items are rated on a 
five-point Likert-type scale ranging from 0 to 4, corresponding to “Never,” 
“Occasionally,” “Sometimes,” “Often” and “Very Often.” Several studies have 
since the publication of the DEX found high internal consistency (Cronbach’s 
alpha >0.90) when the scale was regarded unidimensionally (Bennett et al., 
2005; McGuire et al., 2014). No cut-off scores have been established.
Colour-Word Interference Test 3 (CWIT3). The third condition of the CWIT 
from the Delis-Kaplan Executive Function System (Delis et al., 2001) is primarily a 
measure of inhibitory control (MacLeod, 1991). The task relies on the Stroop 
paradigm and requires participants to say the name of the colour with which 
different coloured words are written, thus foregoing the automatic response 
of reading the words (Stroop, 1935). Standardized scaled scores (normative M  
= 10, SD = 3) adjusted for age were used for completion time (CWIT3-T) and 
number of errors (CWIT3-E). A cut-off of ≤3 scaled scores (≤−2 z-scores below 
normative means) was applied as indicative of impairment.
Trail Making Test (TMT). The TMT assesses processing speed, attention, 
visual scanning and EF (Reitan, 1958; Reitan & Wolfson, 1985). The first part 
(TMT-A) requires connecting numbered circles in a sequence and primarily 
measures visuomotor processing speed and attention. The second part (TMT- 
B) asks of participants to connect numbers in ascending and letters in alphabe­
tical order while alternating between the two tasks, placing additional demands 
NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL REHABILITATION 
5
on cognitive flexibility and working memory. Standardized z-scores (normative 
M = 0, SD = 1) of total completion time adjusted for age and educational level 
were used (Tombaugh, 2004). A cut-off of ≤−2 z-scores below normative 
means was applied as indicative of impairment.
Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS). The HADS (Zigmond & 
Snaith, 1983) consists of 14 items, equally divided into two sub-scales assessing 
symptoms of anxiety (HADS-A) and depression (HADS-D). The items are rated on 
a four-point Likert-type scale ranging from 0 to 3, resulting in possible score 
ranges between 0–21. Scores above 7 on each subscale are considered clinically 
relevant (Bjelland et al., 2002).
Statistical analyzes
Before merging the cohorts, we conducted a Wilcoxon rank-sum test to inves­
tigate whether MI and OHCA participants differed in DEX total scores. Variables 
were examined for the assumptions of parametric analysis visually using Q–Q 
plots, histograms and by Shapiro–Wilk tests. We calculated demographic and 
descriptive statistics, presented as means (M) and standard deviations (SD), 
median and quartile 1 to quartile 3 (Q1–Q3), or numbers (n) and proportion 
(%). There were no substitutions of missing data.
To evaluate the structural validity of the DEX, we first conducted a confirmatory 
factor analysis (CFA) based on the one-factor model suggested by Pedrero-Pérez 
et al. (2015). Although we did tentatively conduct a CFA based on the three-factor 
model suggested by Simblett and Bateman (2011), a comparison of the two 
models indicated that the unidimensional solution provided a better fit, as 
reflected in lower RMSEA values. To handle the ordinal nature of data, the par­
ameters were estimated using the weighted least squares means and variance 
adjusted (WLSMV) method, which is based on a polychromic correlation matrix. 
The WLSMV does not assume normally distributed variables and is appropriate 
for ordinal data such as Likert scales (Li, 2016). Due to the low number of obser­
vations of the higher ratings of “Sometimes,” “Often” and “Very often” reported on 
the DEX in our sample, these categories were collapsed for the CFA. Goodness-of- 
fit indices followed suggested cut-offs (Hu & Bentler, 1999) and include the χ2 test 
with the respective p value; Root Mean Square Error of Approximation (RMSEA, 
values <.06 indicate good fit); Tucker-Lewis Index (TLI, values >0.95 suggest 
good fit); Comparative Fit Index (CFI, values >0.95 suggest good fit), and Standar­
dized Root Mean Residual (SRMR, <0.08 is considered acceptable fit). Ordinal 
alpha and ordinal omega (ω) was used as a measure of internal consistency 
due to the non-parametric nature of the DEX (McNeish, 2018). Cronbach’s 
alpha (α) was additionally calculated to allow for comparisons to previous studies.
Spearman’s rho (rs) was used to assess associations between the DEX, the per­
formance-based tests TMT and CWIT3, and the HADS. Correlation coefficients 
(rs) ranging from 0.10–0.29 were considered small, 0.30–0.49 as medium, and 
6 
A. VIG ET AL.
0.50 or higher as strong (Cohen, 1988). A Bonferroni correction was applied to 
account for multiple comparisons.
Self- and informant rater agreement was assessed by Wilcoxon signed-rank 
test for matched samples. A score as a proxy for potential self-awareness was 
calculated by subtracting total scores on the DEX-Self from those on the DEX- 
Informant, akin to previous studies (Barker et al., 2011; Chan, 2001). Participants 
were then split into two groups based on standardized TMT-B scores being 
equal to or below a z-score of −2, indicative of EF impairment of clinical signifi­
cance. The TMT-B was chosen for stratification due to prior studies showing sen­
sitivity in capturing EF impairment in the target population (Blennow 
Nordström et al., 2023). A Wilcoxon rank sum test was performed to compare 
the groups based on their self-awareness scores on the DEX.
All analyzes were performed with R Statistical Software (v4.1.1; R Core Team, 
2021). Confirmatory factor analyzes (CFA) were conducted using the Lavaan 
package (v0.6-13; Rosseel, 2012). We applied an alpha of <0.05, two-sided, for 
all analyzes.
Results
Sample characteristics
One hundred and six individuals with prior OHCA and 90 individuals with prior 
MI completed the DEX. In conclusion, 196 participants completed the DEX, 
together with their informants (N = 179). See flowchart for details on participant 
inclusion (Supplementary Figure). Demographic details are outlined in Table 1.
With regards to days at hospital after the cardiac event, 51% (n = 100) spent 
10 days or more in care and 43% (n = 84) were discharged after less than 10 days 
with missing data accounting for the remaining 6% (n = 12). At the 7 month 
follow up, 99% (n = 194) of participants were living at home. With regards to 
occupational status, 48% (n = 94) were working and 35% (n = 68) were retired 
due to age. According to self-reported health queries, 70% (n = 137) of partici­
pants were receiving treatment for hypertension and 15% (n = 30) had diabetes.

Structural validity and reliability of the DEX
The initial CFA of the hypothesized one-factor model showed questionable 
model fit, as none of the goodness-of-fit indices reached acceptable levels, 
although they were close: RMSEA = 0.071, CFI = 0.94, TLI = 0.93 and SRMR =  
0.09. An inspection of the residual correlation matrix and modification indices 
revealed a problem with correlated error variances between item 13 and 20. 
Since both items indicate a lack of concern or indifference towards one’s own 
conduct, the residual variances were allowed to correlate in a revised model. 
The revised model demonstrated improved model fit according to all goodness 
of fit indices. Fit indices all approached levels of good fit with RMSEA = 0.062,

Correlations to neuropsychological tests and measures of anxiety and 
depression
Correlations are shown in Table 5. On the neuropsychological measures, only 
the TMT-B correlated significantly with DEX-Self scores (rs −0.21). We found 
additional weak but significant associations between the DEX-Informant and 
measures of EF and processing speed (rs −0.21 and −0.25). Associations 
between the DEX-Self and measures of anxiety and depression were strong 
(rs 0.61) whereas the associations between these same measures and the 
DEX-Informant were moderate (HADS-A: rs 0.45; HADS-D: rs 0.41).

Relationship between the DEX-Self and DEX-Informant
There was no significant difference between total scores on the DEX-Self and 
the DEX-Informant based on the Wilcoxon signed-rank test (p = 0.924). The dis­
crepancy between DEX-Self and DEX-Informant scores between participants 
who scored below the threshold for clinically significant EF impairment on 
the TMT-B (n = 27) and those in the normal range (n = 147) was significant (p  
= .001), see Figure 2. In those impaired, DEX-Self scores were Mdn (Q1–Q3) =  
13 (8.5–19), while DEX-Informant scores were Mdn (Q1–Q3) = 19 (12.5–28.5). In 
those unimpaired, DEX-Self scores were Mdn (Q1–Q3) = 11 (5–18.5), while DEX- 
Informant scores were Mdn (Q1–Q3) = 10 (4–18.5).
Discussion
This is one of few studies that have evaluated the psychometric properties of 
the DEX, considering the ordinal nature of data (Azouvi et al., 2015; Chan & 
Bode, 2008; McGuire et al., 2014; Pedrero-Pérez et al., 2015; Simblett & 
Bateman, 2011; Simblett et al., 2012). The hypothesized unidimensional latent 
structure was supported, with good internal consistency reliability. In addition, 
we found that there were weak but significant associations between DEX and 
common performance-based measures of EF, while measures of anxiety and 
depression correlated strongly with the DEX. We found significant discrepancies

11
Using a confirmatory factor analytic approach, we found support for the uni­
dimensionality of the DEX. While our baseline model did not exhibit good 
model fit, goodness-of-fit indices reached established cut-offs when we let 
the error variances for two items correlate in our revised model. Common pro­
blems when finding correlating residuals can be that a given scale is multidi­
mensional, or that items within the scale overlap and are therefore 
redundant. In our case, we found that the item content for two items (question 
13 and 20) was overlapping. When taking this into account and letting the error 
variances correlate, model fit improved significantly, supporting our notion that 
the DEX is unidimensional. Furthermore, we did not find any indications that the 
scale should be considered multidimensionally based on modification indices, 
factor loadings or correlated errors. Previous literature on the factorial structure 
of the DEX has however been inconclusive, which could be attributed to sample 
effects and the use of parametric statistical methods (e.g., Burgess et al., 1998;

A. VIG ET AL.
Hellebrekers et al., 2017; Simblett & Bateman, 2011). Although our results align 
with studies which applied statistical analyzes suitable for ordinal and non-nor­
mally distributed data (McGuire et al., 2014; Pedrero-Pérez et al., 2015), we do 
acknowledge that a three-factor solution using Rasch analysis has been found 
as well (Simblett & Bateman, 2011). Building further evidence on the factorial 
structure is therefore warranted. Clinicians and researchers may also consider 
using and evaluating the revised version of the scale which has attempted to 
improve measurement precision and has shown promising psychometric qual­
ities (DEX-R; Simblett et al., 2017; Wakely et al., 2022). Unfortunately, the DEX-R 
was not available to us at the time of data collection.
Our finding that ratings on the DEX correlate only weakly with performance 
on common neuropsychological tests of EF is in keeping with evidence from 
several clinical groups, including OHCA (Blennow Nordström et al., 2022; 
Jensen et al., 2024). Individuals with executive difficulties in everyday life – 
such as those resulting from acquired brain injury – may still perform within 
the normal range on neuropsychological tests (Gioia & Isquith, 2004; Shallice 
& Burgess, 1991). Discrepancies between rating-scale and performance-based 
test results has been attributed to factors such as the lack of consensus of the 
definition of EF (Jurado & Rosselli, 2007), the absence of process-pure EF 
measurements (i.e., task-impurity problem; Burgess, 1997), and differing 
demands of the testing situation and everyday environment (Chaytor & Schmit­
ter-Edgecombe, 2003). Furthermore, rating scales and performance-based 
assessments may to some extent measure different aspects of EF abilities 
(Schmitter-Edgecombe et al., 2020). Relatedly, the effectiveness of compensa­
tory strategies and the varying demands of everyday life are thought to 
shape ratings on self- and informant-reports. The weak but significant corre­
lation between our measure of processing speed and the DEX-Informant may 
reflect a general mismatch between participants’ and informants’ view of the 
challenges that arise in everyday life. In our study, associations between per­
formance-based EF tests and DEX informant-ratings were generally somewhat 
stronger than those with self-reports, underscoring the importance of including 
raters with different perspectives when evaluating executive dysfunction.
Unexpectedly, our sample of individuals with a previous cardiac event, along 
with their informants, reported less severe dysexecutive symptoms on the DEX 
compared to non-clinical samples from the general population (Burgess et al., 
1998; Chan, 2001; Pedrero-Pérez et al., 2015). Given that a large portion of the 
sample consisted of older individuals – an age group typically associated with 
EF decline (De Luca et al., 2003; Ferguson et al., 2021) – these low scores 
were surprising. Although age was not examined in detail due to the limited 
number of younger participants, we observed that younger individuals had 
higher DEX scores, indicating slightly more dysexecutive symptoms compared 
to older participants. One possible explanation is the age-related decline in 
negative affect, which has been suggested to reduce the perception of 
NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL REHABILITATION 
13
executive dysfunction and lead to lower scores on the DEX (Gerstorf et al., 2008). 
Younger participants may also be inclined to experience more challenges 
related to EF impairment due to higher demands in everyday life.
Notably, OHCA survivors tend to report fewer subjective cognitive com­
plaints than would be expected based on performance-based tests (Steinbusch 
et al., 2017). While our broad group-level analysis did not indicate clinical levels 
of cognitive impairment, more detailed assessments within this cohort have 
identified objectively verified EF impairments in a subgroup of individuals, 
most of whom exhibit mild to borderline deficits (Blennow Nordström et al., 
2023). Another proposed explanation is, therefore, a response shift following 
a life-threatening event, where individuals adjust their perception of cognitive 
difficulties, leading to fewer reported cognitive complaints (van Gils et al., 
2022). This phenomenon may also be relevant for individuals recovering from 
acute MI and could have had an influence on the total scores of the DEX in 
our study. An item-level analysis showed the largest floor effects on Item 3 (Con­
fabulation) on both the DEX-Self and the DEX-Informant, aligning with previous 
findings in other populations (Azouvi et al., 2015; Cederlund et al., 2010). Con­
fabulation may be less commonly encountered than other EF impairment but 
can be a major and highly disabling symptom. Consequently, the low self- 
and informant-rated DEX total scores suggest that the scale may have limit­
ations in detecting milder forms of executive dysfunction, as well as certain 
symptoms such as confabulation, of which individuals are typically unaware.
The DEX-Self showed strong associations with measures of anxiety and 
depression, while the DEX-Informant showed moderate associations, consistent 
with previous findings (Azouvi et al., 2015). We hypothesize that the magnitude 
of the relationships might be explained by item-level content overlap between 
the HADS and the DEX. For instance, some items on the DEX ask raters to assess 
feelings of lethargy, unenthusiasm, difficulty in decision-making, and restless­
ness, symptoms which also characterize anxiety and depression (American Psy­
chiatric Association, 2022). Such analyzes were beyond the scope of our study 
but may warrant further research.
We found no significant difference in total scores between the self- and infor­
mant-raters in the overall sample, in contrast with some previous studies. 
However, those with EF impairment as identified by performance-based tests, 
rated their difficulties as significantly less severe than informants did. This 
suggests that the discrepancy between self- and informant scores on the DEX 
captures impaired self-awareness in those with greater EF impairment. The 
inconsistencies in previous studies may therefore be due to variabilities in popu­
lation characteristics. Using both the self- and informant-rated DEX thus shows 
promise in identifying and evaluating impaired self-awareness over time, which 
could be valuable in clinical management of patients undergoing neurorehabil­
itation (Prigatano, 2024).
14 
A. VIG ET AL.
This study contributes to the existing literature by examining the psycho­
metric properties of the DEX in a novel sample, consisting of individuals with 
a prior cardiac event. A key strength is the use of statistical methods suited 
for ordinal and non-normally distributed data. Additionally, the availability of 
both self- and informant ratings provided insight into potential self-awareness 
impairments. Combined with performance-based test results, we have shown 
that EF impairment is related to altered self-awareness of executive dysfunction. 
To enhance clinical relevance and aid clinicians in selecting appropriate assess­
ments, we included measures of anxiety and depression.
The study also has limitations. First, although most participants allotted the 
DEX-Informant to family members, we lack detailed knowledge about the infor­
mants such as their familiarity with participants’ premorbid functioning, which 
may influence rater accuracy (Barker et al., 2011). Second, additional individual 
factors such as prior psychiatric or neurodevelopmental diagnoses were not 
available to us. Third, the sample exhibits an uneven sex distribution, a conse­
quence of men being overrepresented in having cardiovascular disease (Roth et 
al., 2017). Fourth, because most cognitive recovery occurs within the first three 
months after cardiac arrest, some individuals may have reported higher levels of 
impairment if assessed more closely in time following the cardiac event (Byron- 
Alhassan et al., 2021; Moulaert et al., 2017). Fifth, the sample may not be entirely 
representative of the population, as participants with cognitive impairment fol­
lowing OHCA may have been slightly underrepresented (Blennow Nordström et 
al., 2023), and we were not able to investigate measurement invariance and 
differential item functioning between MI and OHCA participants due to 
sample size constraints.
The DEX is a brief, easily administered tool for assessing dysexecutive symp­
toms in adults at risk of EF impairment due to cardiovascular burden. While DEX 
ratings show some association with objectively measured EF even when symp­
toms are subclinical, self-reports should be used cautiously, as individuals may 
both under- and overreport symptom severity. To obtain more comprehensive 
EF assessments, self-ratings should be used in combination with neuropsycho­
logical testing and informant reports. We also recommend screening for anxiety 
and depression, as both are related to EF as measured by the DEX. The psycho­
metric inconsistencies observed in prior studies highlight the need for caution 
when using the DEX as a quantitative measure, and the absence of established 
cut-off scores complicates the interpretation of total scores. Nonetheless, we 
propose that the DEX is a useful tool that provides a rough estimate of EF 
impairment and can serve as a starting point for further evaluation.
